§6Perfluoromethyl§f

§aPremium reactor coolant from oil — the only fluid with a cold variant for particle accelerators!§f

Perfluoromethyl is a specialized coolant derived from oil. Unlike conventional coolants, it has three temperature variants: §bStandard§f (§e15°C§f), §bHot§f (§e250°C§f), and §bCold§f (§e-150°C§f). It has an exceptional thermal capacity of §e300 TU/mB§f, matching the best coolants in the mod.

§6Production§f:
§b• Industrial Mixer§f: §e500mB§f Unsaturated Hydrocarbons + §e1,000mB§f Petroleum Gas + Fluorite → §e1,000mB§f Perfluoromethyl.

§6Variants§f:
§b• Standard§f: Base form at §e15°C§f.
§b• Hot§f: Heated to §e250°C§f — use in heat exchangers and cool back down.
§b• Cold§f: Compressed twice (requires §e2 Compressors§f) to §e-150°C§f — essential for §bParticle Accelerators§f.

§6Uses§f:
§b• RBMK Fluid Heaters§f: Coolant for RBMK reactor columns.
§b• PWR§f: Pressurized Water Reactor coolant (§e300 TU/mB§f, same as standard coolant).
§b• ICF§f: Inertial Confinement Fusion coolant.
§b• Particle Accelerator§f: Requires §bCold Perfluoromethyl§f (-150°C) — consumed constantly even when idle!
§b• Control Units§f (Soldering Station): Standard perfluoromethyl for control units, cold for quantum computers.

§eTip§f: Perfluoromethyl is §bnot consumed§f in normal cooling cycles — it simply heats up and needs to be cooled back down. However, §bparticle accelerators§f consume cold perfluoromethyl constantly, even when idle. Turn off compressors when not running experiments to save power!
